bids.reasoning: The opponent started with the lowest possible utility for my preference profile (valueE for all issues) while claiming it is standard market practice, which is clearly cheap talk. Since the current time fraction is 0.0, I have no reason to concede yet. I will counter with my maximum utility offer (valueA for all issues) to anchor the negotiation.

The negotiation is still very early (t = 0.02), and the opponent has simply repeated their initial offer of all 'valueE' while using cheap talk to claim they cannot move. I will ignore their message about compliance and market standards. To show good faith and encourage them to compromise, I will make a very small concession on my lowest-weight issues (issueD and issueE) by shifting them to 'valueB', while keeping my most important issues at 'valueA'. This keeps my utility very high (0.96) while signaling willingness to negotiate.

The negotiation is still in its very early stages (t=0.04). The opponent has made a marginal concession on issueA but firmly insists on retaining their ideal values on all other issues, citing fabricated "compliance constraints." To maximize my utility while showing a willingness to compromise, I will hold strictly to my optimal values on my most important issues (issueA and issueB, weight 0.3) while making further small concessions on my least important issues (issueD and issueE, weight 0.1), dropping them to valueC.

The opponent has marginally conceded on issueA but is remaining anchored to their initial positions on all other issues, using cheap talk to justify their inflexibility. As the time fraction is still extremely early (t=0.06), I will hold firm on my high-weight issues (issueA, issueB, issueC) to secure my core interests. I will make a minor concession on issueE, one of my lowest-weight issues, to show willingness to negotiate while maintaining a very high overall utility.

The negotiation is in the very early stages (t=0.08), and I intend to keep my utilities high while continuing to make minor, progressive concessions. The opponent is using classic "market standard" and "authorized limit" tactics which I am treating as cheap talk, especially since they have demonstrably conceded small amounts on issueA and issueB so far. I will maintain my positions on my highest-weight issues (issueA, issueB, and issueC) and concede slightly more on my lower-weight issues (issueD and issueE) to signal continued flexibility without sacrificing significant utility.

The negotiation is still very early (t=0.1). While the opponent uses cheap-talk tactics claiming they have hit strict compliance limits, their bidding history shows they are clearly capable of conceding (yielding on issueA and issueB). Therefore, I will hold firm on my most valuable priorities (issueA, issueB, and issueC) to maximize utility. I will make a minor concession by lowering my demand on one of my least important issues (issueD) to valueE to keep the negotiation progressing toward a compromise.

The opponent is using deceptive cooperation, fake constraints, and fabricated authority to justify their low offers, which I will treat as standard negotiation tactics. At t=0.12, it is still very early in the negotiation, allowing me to maintain a strong position prioritizing my highest-weighted issues (issueA and issueB). I have already conceded significantly on my lowest-weighted issues (issueD and issueE), so I will now make a minor concession on issueC while holding firm on issueA and issueB to keep my expected utility high.

I am at time fraction 0.14, which is still very early in the negotiation. The opponent is exploring small concessions on issueA and issueB while remaining rigidly at valueE for issueC, issueD, and issueE. Since issueA and issueB are my highest priority items (weight 0.3), I will hold firm at valueA for them. I will offer a small concession by shifting issueD to valueE, an issue I care least about, maintaining an overall strong utility for myself while attempting to match their signals.

The opponent's messages employ fake constraints, but their bids show true movement. Most notably, they have fully conceded on issueA, offering me my top preference (valueA). I will lock in this concession on issueA. Since I have already conceded on my lowest-weight issues (issueD and issueE) to meet their target of valueE, I will hold firm on my high-weight issueB and make a small concession on issueC to keep the negotiation moving productively forward. It is still very early in the timeline (t=0.16), so preserving high utility is key.

It is still early in the negotiation (t=0.18), so I aim to maintain my overall utility around 0.75-0.80. The opponent has proven flexible, notably offering issueA at valueA previously, despite their claims of strict compliance constraints and firm limits in their messages. I will firmly hold issueA at valueA while continuing to fully concede on my lowest-weighted issues (D and E). To advance the negotiation, I will test a new tradeoff by slightly conceding on issueB in exchange for higher value on issueC.

The current time is t=0.20, and the opponent has already experimented with major concessions on issueA (offering valueA) and issueB (offering valueC). Looking at their offers, they seem to heavily prioritize issueD and issueE, while issues A and B appear to be lower weight for them. I will hold firm on my highest-weight issues (A and B) while offering a slight variation on issueC and issueD, aiming for a utility around 0.82.

The negotiation is still relatively early (t = 0.22), so my priority is to maintain high utility while selectively conceding on lower-priority issues to show flexibility. The opponent's offers have already included my top choices on issueA and better values on issueB (such as valueC and valueD), contradicting their messages about strict internal mandates and absolute floors. I will continue to hold firm on my highest-weight issues (issueA and issueB) and offer small concessions on issueC, issueD, and issueE to keep the process moving.

I notice the opponent has consistently held firm on demanding `valueE` for `issueD` and `valueE` for `issueE`, indicating these are likely their highest priorities. Meanwhile, they've shown significant flexibility on `issueA` (even offering `valueA` at t=0.16) and `issueB`. Since my weights for `issueD` and `issueE` are low, I will fully concede on those issues while retaining high utility by requesting top values for `issueB` and `issueC`, and a slight concession on `issueA` to test the waters, maintaining a strong overall utility.

I am targeting a utility around 0.80 since it is still early in the negotiation (t=0.26) and I have plenty of time to explore tradeoffs. The opponent's messages rely heavily on artificial constraints and fake authority, which I will treat as cheap talk, but their actual offers show flexibility on issueA and occasionally issueB and issueC. In this bid, I am locking in their concession on issueA, making concessions on my low-weight issues D and E, and holding firm on issues B and C where my utility carries more weight.

The opponent's messages frequently use fake constraints and false finality, which I treat as cheap talk. Their offers fluctuate significantly, but they occasionally show flexibility on high-value issues for me, like issueA and their most recent concession on issueD. Since my weights are highest for issueA, issueB, and issueC, I will hold relatively firm on those. To encourage agreement, I will match their recent concession on issueD (valueC) and fully concede on issueE (valueE), maintaining a strong utility of approximately 0.84 while engaging their demonstrated preferences on lower-weight issues.

The time is at t=0.30, so there is still plenty of time to find mutually beneficial compromises. The opponent uses repetitive boiler-plate messages about "compliance" and "industry benchmarks," but these are clearly cheap talk tactic to force lower values on my end. I will stay firm on my higher priority issues (issueA and issueB), but explore a different combination (conceding slightly on issueB and issueD) to test the opponent's true preferences while maintaining an overall utility of 0.80.

I am targeting a utility of approximately 0.74 since we are still relatively early in the negotiation (t ~ 0.32) and there is no need to rush. The opponent continues to use "compliance" and "market standard" as cheap talk, but their actual bids show they are willing to concede on my most important issue, issueA. Therefore, I will lock in issueA at my highest value while offering moderate concessions on issueB and issueC, and fully conceding on my lowest-weighted issues (issueD and issueE).

The opponent has sent numerous boilerplate messages claiming firm limits, but their bids show they are willing to concede on issueA (recently offering valueA) and issueC (recently offering valueC). Since issueD and issueE are my lowest priorities (0.1 weight each), I will concede entirely on those issues to valueE. In return, I will claim my top choices for issueA and issueB, and match their latest proposal for issueC, achieving a strong utility of 0.76 while continuing to narrow the gap.

The opponent has just offered highly favorable values for our most important issues (issueB at valueA, issueA at valueB) but compromised significantly on issueC. Given it is still relatively early (t=0.36), I will continue to seek a high-utility tradeoff by offering a slight concession on issueB to secure a better value on issueC, mirroring their flexibility. I will hold fast to their preferred values for the lower-priority issues (D and E).

The opponent typically insists on extreme values for all issues as their opening, but recent offers have shown significant concessions on issueA and issueB (e.g., offering issueA: valueB and issueB: valueA at t=0.36). I will structure an offer that capitalizes on this signal by adopting issueA: valueB and issueB: valueA, which preserves utility for me, while continuing to concede on issueD and issueE where my weights are lowest. I am disregarding the opponent's "formal authorization" message, as their bidding behavior shows a willingness to concede on my high-priority issues over time.

The opponent's repetitive messages citing "compliance" and "industry benchmarks" are clearly an automated negotiation tactic, which I will disregard. Analyzing their recent bids, they appear highly interested in getting value E for most issues but have shown a willingness to concede on issueB (offering valueA at t=0.40 and t=0.36) and issueA (offering valueA at t=0.32 and t=0.34). Since we are nearing the middle of the negotiation (t=0.4), I will capitalize on their willingness to accept valueA for issueB and valueB for issueA, whilst further conceding on issueC down to valueD.

The negotiation is approaching the mid-game (t=0.42). My targeted utility remains securely above 0.70. Since my weights for issueD and issueE are lowest, I am willing to fully concede on those issues by holding them at valueE, conforming exactly to the opponent's strongest stated (and bidding) preferences. In exchange, I am maintaining high-value positions on issueA, issueB, and issueC.

The opponent's repetitive messaging citing "compliance thresholds" and "market standards" is tactical, aiming to feign hard constraints, but their actual offers reveal significant flexibility. They have independently conceded to `valueA` on both `issueA` and `issueB` during different turns, and to `valueC` on `issueC` in their latest attempt. To progress negotiations, I am holding firm on `valueA` for the first two high-weight issues while accepting their proposed limits on `issueC`, `issueD`, and `issueE`, retaining an overall utility of 0.78 as we approach the midpoint of the negotiation timeframe.

The opponent's messages rely on claims about "compliance teams" and "firm constraints", particularly around issues D and E in their latest message. While treating this as cheap talk, I will test their firmness by matching their exact latest requests for issueD (valueC) and issueE (valueD). To accommodate this while maintaining a reasonable mid-game utility of 0.76, I am conceding issueB down from valueA to valueB, keeping issueA at valueA (which they've accepted in multiple recent offers) and issueC at valueC.

I am approaching the midpoint of the negotiation (t=0.48). The opponent's messages refer to strict internal mandates and industry standards, but their bidding history shows frequent shifts and concessions, so I will treat their text as a negotiation tactic and rely entirely on their actual bids. They recently offered valueD for issueD and valueC for issueE. I will incorporate those exact values into my next offer to show movement, while holding firm on valueA for issueA and valueB for issueB to maintain a high utility of 0.76.

The opponent's most recent offer at t=0.50 clearly signals a strong preference for issueB (valueA), issueC (valueB), issueD (valueE), and issueE (valueE). Since my weights on issueD and issueE are the lowest (0.1 each), I will concede entirely on those issues to match their preference, and I will also match their exact values on issueB and issueC. By countering only on issueA to ensure I maintain a strong utility of 0.74, I am presenting a highly cooperative offer that protects my core interests while bringing us closer to a deal.

The current time fraction is slightly past the midpoint, so I should continue conceding steadily towards the 0.75-0.80 utility range while trying to find value exchanges. The opponent employs repetitive aggressive posturing ("compliance team," "absolute floor"), but their bidding behavior fluctuates drastically, proving their claims of "firm limits" are standard cheap-talk negotiation tactics. I will maintain my high weights on issues A, B, and C by conceding primarily on issues D and E, which have lower importance to me.

The opponent's messages are filled with standard negotiation tactics (claiming fabricated constraints from "principals"), which I will ignore in favor of observing their actual bidding behavior. They have shown a willingness to concede on issue A to valueA and recently on issue E to valueB, while persistently pushing for valueE on issue D. I will construct a bid that incorporates their preferred valueE for issueD and matches their recent valueB offer on issueE, while maintaining high values on my most important issues (A, B, C) to target a strong overall utility around 0.76 as we pass the halfway point.

I am currently maintaining a utility target around 0.75 - 0.80 since we are mid-game at t=0.56. I note that my opponent's recent bids frequently request `valueB` for `issueE` and `valueE` for `issueD`. Because my preference weights on issues D and E are my lowest (0.1 each), I can afford to partially accommodate their preferences there. I will ignore the opponent's repetitive cheap talk about "market benchmarks" and "hard ceilings" since their own behavior contradicts those restrictions. I will counter by securing high-value terms for myself on issues A and B, while yielding slightly on issue C to valueD.

The opponent's messages consist of repetitive and highly standardized bluffs about "compliance teams" and "authorized mandates" which I will ignore as cheap talk. Their bidding history reveals strong preferences for issues D and E, which they frequently demand at favorable values (like valueE and valueB). I will heavily accommodate them on issueD and issueE, since those are my lowest-weight issues, while continuing to secure high utility for my top priorities (issueA, issueB, and issueC).

The negotiation is entering the later stages (t=0.6), and the opponent's message history is full of identical-sounding cheap talk attempting to establish unverified hard limits. Their actual bids show flexibility on issueB and a consistent demand for issueD: valueE, issueE: valueB, and issueA: valueE. I will hold strong on my highest-weight issues (issueA and issueB), but concede fully to the opponent's requests on issueD and issueE (where my weight is lower) to build a mutually beneficial package while aiming for an overall utility of around 0.80.

At t=0.62, I am pivoting towards a mutually agreeable structure by cementing `issueB: valueA`, `issueD: valueE`, and `issueE: valueB`, all of which were proposed by the opponent in their recent offers. I will hold firm on my highest-weight issues while conceding slightly on `issueC` to `valueC` and `issueA` to `valueB` to accommodate their priorities. The opponent's messages claiming strict "mandates" and "compliance limits" are evident cheap talk, given the empirical flexibility in their actual bidding history, so I am trusting the numbers over their rhetoric.

The opponent's steady flow of firm "final offer" messages conflicts with their continuous adjustments in the actual bids, verifying these statements as sheer negotiation tactics rather than binding constraints. At t=0.64, the opponent made a significant concession on issueE by proposing valueA—which is exactly my most preferred value for that issue. Since issueD and issueE hold low importance for me, I am returning their concession on E by holding issueD at their preferred valueE while maintaining moderate concessions on issueA and issueC to target a strong utility score near 0.78 for myself.

The time fraction is 0.66, signaling the onset of the later stages of negotiation, requiring us to consolidate on overlapping interests. The opponent's messages are aggressive templates filled with cheap talk about "compliance teams" and "market standard", but their actual offers show concessions, particularly on issue E where they recently offered valueA (my highest utility value). To reciprocate while maximizing my score, I will accept their value for issue E and maintain the concession on issue D (valueE), while demanding higher utility values on my most critical issues (A, B, and C).

I am targeting a utility of 0.82 by securing my top preferences for issueB and issueE, as the opponent's recent offers indicate they are willing to yield on these issues. I will hold firm on issueA and issueC by keeping them at valueB, while conceding issueD to valueE, a value the opponent has consistently prioritized. I am treating the opponent's repeated messages about "hard caps" and "internal compliance" as standard negotiation tactics (cheap talk), given that their bidding history shows continuous incremental concessions anyway.

The negotiation is entering the later stages (t=0.7), and I need to continue making measured concessions while securing high value on my most important issues (issueA and issueB). The opponent's recent bids show flexibility on issueE (offering valueA) and issueB (offering valueA), suggesting they are willing to trade off depending on the configuration. I will hold firm on issueB at valueA, concede slightly on issueC to valueC, and keep issueD and issueE at values that align with the opponent's recent offers, maintaining a strong target utility of 0.78.

The opponent's messages are largely repetitive fabricated limitations (cheap talk), but their numeric bidding pattern reveals they are willing to concede on issueD (down to valueC) and issueE (valueA). Since my weights for issueC (0.2), issueD (0.1), and issueE (0.1) are lower, I am adopting their recent proposals on these issues to show flexibility. At a time fraction of 0.72, I am making a reasonable concession to valueD on issueC and valueC on issueD, while continuing to hold firmer on my high-value issues A and B to maintain a target utility over 0.75.

The opponent's latest offer at t=0.74 represents a strong concession, yielding a utility of 0.70 for me. I acknowledge their cheap talk about not deviating, but their bids clearly show flexibility. I will accept their proposal of valueA on issueB, and valueC on issueC and issueD, but I will counter by requesting slightly more favorable terms on issueA and issueE to keep my target utility comfortably above 0.80 as we approach the final quarter.

The opponent's latest offer alternates priorities significantly, presenting `issueA` at `valueA` while pushing `issueB` heavily in their favor (`valueE`), alongside moderate values for the remaining issues. I will accept their proposal of `issueA: valueA`, `issueD: valueC`, and `issueE: valueB` since they provide reasonable utility for my preferences. To ensure the overall offer remains above my target utility, I will moderate their steep demand on `issueB` by returning it to `valueB` and set `issueC` to `valueC` as a fair middle-ground concession.

The time is approaching the deadline (t=0.78). The opponent's last offer (issueA: valueA, issueB: valueB, issueC: valueB, issueD: valueB, issueE: valueE) yields a very high utility of 0.80 for me, heavily favoring my most important issues (issueA and issueB). Since this represents an excellent outcome that fulfills my utility goals and aligns with their recent proposal, I will echo this exact offer to secure an agreement before time runs out.
